prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. He knocked this out in about 20 minutes over lunch. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

# Grade: 8/10

## What Works Really Well

**Strong narrative arc & voice.** This feels authentic—like a real person reflecting, not someone trying to sound profound. The specific details (the "pick and roll" confusion, the kid saying "stay out of the way," Marcus making the team) ground it and make it believable.

**Layered reflection.** It's not just "I failed and learned X." You've got multiple connected insights: driveway vs. competitive play, failure as redirection, and the meta-lesson about not knowing what you don't know. That last paragraph is genuinely good—the realization that you can't identify excellence without experience is a real insight.

**Good pacing.** For 20 minutes and ~410 words, you move efficiently through the story without getting bogged down. The debate pivot is an especially nice touch that shows this isn't just wallowing—it's about growth.

## Room to Improve

**A few telling moments that could show more.** "I was really upset for a while" tells us the emotion, but something more concrete (avoided friends, couldn't watch basketball, etc.) would let us feel it.

**Some phrases are a bit generic.** Lines like "Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things" flatten the lesson into cliché. Earlier in the essay you show this beautifully; trust those scenes to do the work.

**"So yeah" opening the final paragraph** is conversational (fits your voice!) but walks the reflection back slightly when you're about to land something important.

## Bottom Line

This is genuinely impressive for 20 minutes. It has a real voice, actual insight, and isn't trying too hard. One more pass for tightness would push it higher, but it already does the job well.

Let me analyze this essay against the prompt and writing quality criteria.

**Prompt Requirements:**
- Topic: Write about a time you failed and what you learned
- Format: Short personal narrative (~500 words)
- Implied context: This appears to be a student assignment, possibly high school or early college

**What Works:**

1. **Clear narrative arc**: The essay has a solid beginning (summer practice), middle (tryouts failure), and resolution (joining debate). It moves chronologically and logically.

2. **Specific details**: Basketball terminology (pick and roll, fast break), the kid telling him to "stay out of the way," Marcus making the team, the Friday list going up—these concrete details make it vivid and believable.

3. **Honest reflection**: The author shows genuine vulnerability. He admits his self-deception ("I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did") and recognizes his overconfidence.

4. **Multiple layers of learning**: Not just "I learned I wasn't as good as I thought," but also discoveries about casual vs. competitive play, redirecting toward better opportunities, and the nature of self-awareness/knowledge gaps.

5. **Strong closing**: The final paragraph ties it together philosophically—failure as information, not an ending. This elevates it beyond just a personal anecdote.

6. **Good pacing and tone**: Given this was written in 20 minutes, it's well-organized and maintains a conversational but clear voice throughout.

7. **Emotional honesty**: The admission about Marcus making the team, the humiliation of being told to stay out of the way, the disappointment—all feel genuine.

**What Doesn't Work as Well:**

1. **Word count**: The prompt asks for ~500 words. This is probably around 400-420 words. It's a bit short, though not drastically so. The author could have expanded some scenes or added more detail.

2. **Show vs. Tell in places**: Some of the reflection feels a bit summary-ish. 

The phrase "I was really upset for a while" summarizes an emotional state rather than letting me experience it—a few concrete details about what that looked like would make it land harder.

3. **The debate pivot feels slightly rushed**: "I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me." This is the biggest turning point and it gets glossed over. How did that happen? Why debate specifically? Did you try other things? A bit more detail here would strengthen the narrative.

4. **Minor issues**:
   - "Obviously" in the paragraph about the list going up feels a bit flip for an otherwise sincere piece
   - The structure of "I still think about that kid..." in the second-to-last paragraph feels slightly disconnected from the main arc

—it could have been woven into the scrimmage section for better flow.

5. **Clichés**: Some conclusions feel predictable ("failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information"), though they're genuinely felt rather than forced.

Overall, this is a competent essay that handles the assignment well. It demonstrates self-awareness, honest reflection, solid narrative structure, and meaningful insight into the experience. The voice is genuine and engaging, though it lacks exceptional prose or surprising depth. For a 20-minute piece, it's strong work—around 8.5-9/10 for high school level, or 8/10 for college, where it could benefit from more development and originality.

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. He knocked this out in about 20 minutes over lunch. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. He knocked this out in about 20 minutes over lunch. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

**Overall assessment:**

This is a solid personal narrative. For a 20-minute lunch essay, it's quite good. It has:

- Authentic voice
- Specific details
- Meaningful reflection
- Appropriate length

The main weaknesses are:
- Some very minor tonal inconsistencies (the casual asides)
- Could have deeper emotional resonance in places

It's not exceptional (which would require more emotional depth, more vivid detail, or more original insights), but it's definitely above average for a quick assignment.

Let me settle on **7.5/10** because while it executes well, it could use a bit more depth and a slightly fresher conclusion.

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. He knocked this out in about 20 minutes over lunch. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

## What Works Well

**Strong fundamentals**: Your friend nails the core assignment. Clear failure, genuine reflection, real lessons. The arc from overconfidence → humbling experience → unexpected growth is satisfying and honest.

**Tell vs. show**: "I was really upset for a while" or "Marcus made it though, which was hard"—these emotional moments would land harder with concrete details showing us rather than stating it.

**Informal phrasing**: "Obviously," "sounds like a weird pivot," "So yeah"—these feel chatty for an essay. A bit of tightening would help.

**Limited sensory detail**: The basketball scenes could be more vivid. What did it *feel* like being outmatched physically?

---

**Overall**: For a 20-minute effort, this is genuinely strong. It's honest, well-structured, and has real insight. With another 30 minutes of polish and expansion (especially around the debate payoff), this would be an 8.5 or 9.

# Grade: 7.5/10 (B+)

## What Works Really Well

**Strong narrative arc** — Clear progression from setup through failure to unexpected resolution. You actually *care* what happens, which is the hardest part of personal narrative.

**Specific, concrete details** — "Pick and roll," the kid saying "stay out of the way," Marcus making the team, the debate pivot. These make it real instead of generic.

**Genuine voice** — Feels authentic. The vulnerability ("told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did") and the self-aware humor land naturally.

**Meaningful insight** — The "you don't know what you don't know" lesson isn't preachy, and the observation about casual vs. competitive play is solid. He actually *shows* learning by explaining how it pushed him toward debate.

## What Needs Work

**Emotional depth feels rushed** — He mentions being "really upset for a while" but glosses over it. The shame/frustration moment (the scrimmage) deserves more weight since that's the real turning point. Show us what that felt like more vividly.

**The debate pivot is underdeveloped** — This is where the payoff should be strongest, but it's summarized in a couple of sentences. Why did debate click? How was it different? A specific moment or example would make the resolution much stronger.

**Some lazy phrasing** — "Obviously," "So yeah," and "failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information" veer into telling rather than showing. The last one especially feels like a greeting card.

**Marcus subplot disappears** — The comparison to Marcus is emotionally charged but never resolved. Did that change their friendship? More complexity here would deepen the piece.

## Bottom Line

For 20 minutes, this is genuinely impressive. It meets every requirement, has real insight, and sounds like an actual person. With another 10 minutes of revision focusing on the emotional beats and the debate section, this would easily be an 8.5-9.
